title: Decoherence in matter-wave Talbot interference: a hydrodynamic probability-flow analysis
abstract: We investigate the suppression of matter-wave Talbot interference under environmentally induced decoherence. The system is modeled as an atomic beam diffracted by a periodic grating, whose transverse dynamics is described within the paraxial approximation. Environmental coupling is introduced through an effective open-system model that exponentially damps spatial coherences between diffracted components, allowing a continuous interpolation between the coherent Talbot regime and the incoherent far-field diffraction limit. Besides the usual intensity and transverse-momentum distributions, we analyze the local probability flow associated with the diffracted matter wave. The corresponding Bohmian, or hydrodynamic, representation is used here as a diagnostic tool fully equivalent to the standard quantum description, with no additional assumptions beyond the probability current of the paraxial wave field. In the present Talbot geometry, this analysis shows how decoherence progressively suppresses the carpet structure and smooths the transverse-momentum distribution, while the flow may remain organized into channels determined by the grating periodicity. The results illustrate, in a periodic matter-wave Talbot geometry, that the loss of visible interference and the loss of dynamical pathway separation need not occur simultaneously. In particular, flux-channel structures can persist in parameter regimes where multi-slit interference features have already been strongly reduced. This distinction provides a local characterization of decoherence in matter-wave Talbot interferometry and complements previous trajectory-based analyses of coherence loss in simpler interference and confined geometries.
summary: This paper explores how environmental interactions suppress the Talbot effect in matter-wave diffraction. It demonstrates that while interference fringes disappear under decoherence, the underlying organized transport of probability flow can persist. This distinction offers a new way to diagnose the impact of decoherence using local hydrodynamic observables.
main_problem: Investigating how environmentally induced decoherence suppresses matter-wave Talbot interference and determining if the loss of interference visibility occurs simultaneously with the loss of organized probability flow.
main_result: The study finds that the loss of visible interference (global observable) and the loss of dynamical pathway separation (local observable) do not necessarily occur simultaneously; flux-channel structures can persist even after interference features are strongly reduced.
method: A hydrodynamic/Bohmian representation of the paraxial matter-wave equation is used to analyze probability-flow streamlines and intensity distributions.
model_or_system: An atomic beam (monochromatic, non-interacting) diffracted by a periodic grating of Gaussian apertures, modeled using a Lindblad-type master equation with an effective position-localization term to simulate environmental decoherence.
key_observables: Intensity distributions (Talbot carpet), transverse-momentum distributions, local probability-flow streamlines, and transverse momentum fields.
important_parameters: Decoherence strength (Lambda), coherence range (Delta x), Talbot distance (zT), and the transition from coherent to far-field diffraction regimes.
main_assumptions: The system is treated within the paraxial approximation, assuming a monochromatic, non-interacting, and spinless atomic beam.
figures_summary: Figures show the transition of the Talbot carpet and momentum maps under increasing decoherence, the comparison of coherence range to Gaussian width, and the evolution of probability-flow streamlines from organized channels to spreading patterns.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of decoherence in Talbot interference, defines the hydrodynamic/Bohmian framework and the decoherence model, presents numerical results for intensity and momentum maps, analyzes the persistence of flow channels, and concludes with the far-field asymptotic behavior.
why_interesting: It provides a novel local characterization of decoherence by distinguishing between the decay of phase coherence and the decay of dynamical transport organization, which is highly relevant for understanding the quantum-to-classical transition in atom interferometry.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Talbot-like carpet produced by the superposition of a finite periodic array of 50 diffracted Gaussian wave pack- ets and its gradual suppression as the decoherence strength is increased: (a/a’) Λ = 0, (b/b’) Λ = 10−3 mm−1·µm−2, and (c/c’) Λ = 1 mm−1·µm−2. The density plots of the intensity are shown in the left column, while the right column shows the corresponding relative transverse momentum, kx/k0, with k0 = 2π/d. The blue-to-red color code in the left column de- notes increasing normalized intensity. In the right column, the blue-to-red scale denotes negative-to-positive values of kx/k0 in the range −0.5 ≤kx/k0 ≤0.5, with white corresponding to nearly zero transverse momentum.
FIG. 2. Same as Fig. 1, but restricted to a neighborhood of x = 0 in order to highlight the Talbot-like structure for Λ = 0 and its progressive suppression as Λ increases.

FIG. 3. Dependence of the inter-slit coherence range ∆x on the propagation coordinate for various values of the decoher- ence strength: 10−3 mm−1·µm−2 (red solid line), 10−2 mm−1· µm−2 (green solid line), 10−1 mm−1 ·µm−2 (magenta solid line), and 1 mm−1 ·µm−2 (blue solid line). For comparison, the width σz of a single diffracted Gaussian, Eq. (21), is also shown (black solid line; right axis).

FIG. 4. Probability-flow streamlines associated with the three cases represented in Fig. 1: (a) Λ = 0, (b) Λ = 10−3 mm−1·µm−2, and (c) Λ = 1 mm−1·µm−2. A set of 11 streamlines, with evenly distributed initial conditions across each slit width, is used for each opening, giving 550 stream- lines in total. For clarity, the panels are restricted to the central region considered in Fig. 2.
